searches_performed.query: St John's wort cyclosporine transplant rejection CYP3A4 hypericum case report
searches_performed.notes: Multiple documented transplant rejections (heart, kidney, pancreas) from SJW + cyclosporine. Mechanism same as OC interaction (CYP3A4/P-gp induction).

sources.title: Acute heart transplant rejection due to Saint John's wort
sources.population: Two heart transplant recipients on cyclosporine-based immunosuppression
sources.design_notes: Case reports: cyclosporine levels dropped by 49% and significantly in both patients after self-starting SJW. Acute graft rejection developed in both.
sources.key_findings: Documented acute heart transplant rejection attributed to cyclosporine underexposure caused by SJW induction. Rejection resolved with SJW discontinuation + standard rejection treatment; cyclosporine levels returned to therapeutic range.

sources.title: Interaction between cyclosporine and Hypericum perforatum (St John's wort) after organ transplantation
sources.design_notes: Case series. Published in American Journal of Kidney Diseases.
sources.key_findings: Cyclosporine levels decreased 25-62% within 3-4 weeks of SJW initiation. Graft rejection observed in all 4 cases. Discontinuation of SJW returned cyclosporine levels to therapeutic range.

sources.title: St John's wort (Hypericum perforatum): drug interactions and clinical outcomes
sources.design_notes: Narrative review of SJW pharmacovigilance and interaction literature.
sources.key_findings: Catalogues multiple transplant-rejection reports attributed to SJW + cyclosporine. Cites this as one of the most dangerous documented SJW interactions. Explicitly names cyclosporine, tacrolimus, sirolimus as immunosuppressants at risk.

sources.title: Clinical relevance of St John's wort drug interactions revisited
sources.design_notes: Reviews CYP3A4 + P-gp induction mechanisms for hyperforin.
sources.key_findings: Hyperforin is a potent PXR agonist inducing CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein; cyclosporine is a narrow-therapeutic-index CYP3A4 substrate, making the interaction predictable and clinically severe. Low-hyperforin SJW products may not trigger the interaction, but cannot be relied on for transplant patients.

claims.statement: St John's wort substantially reduces cyclosporine blood levels (25-62%) and has caused acute transplant rejection in heart, kidney, and pancreas recipients.

dose_threshold.a: Standard retail SJW doses (600-900 mg/day high-hyperforin) have caused the interaction.
dose_threshold.notes: Multiple case reports across organ types confirm the interaction is not incidental.

claims.statement: The mechanism is hyperforin-mediated induction of CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, accelerating cyclosporine metabolism and efflux.

dose_threshold.a: Hyperforin content >1 mg/day considered high-risk; low-hyperforin products may not trigger but are not reliable for transplant patients.
dose_threshold.b: Applies to all CYP3A4-metabolized immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, sirolimus also at risk).
dose_threshold.notes: Same mechanism as SJW + OC interaction, with worse consequences given narrow therapeutic window.

claims.statement: St John's wort is contraindicated in all transplant recipients on cyclosporine, tacrolimus, or sirolimus.

dose_threshold.notes: Non-reversible harm (transplant rejection) justifies absolute contraindication.

reviewer_notes: Original entry was DANGER, 28 words, 1/3 specific. Severity holds at DANGER: multiple case reports of graft rejection (non-reversible harm), strong mechanism, broad transplant-population consensus (all major transplant centers contraindicate SJW). This is one of the cleanest DANGER pairs in the list.
